preFix.title: Multi-layer nuno felt colour blending
preFix.subtitle: Layering differently coloured merino roving over silk organza to produce gradients and marled effects in a felted scarf
preFix.excerpt: Felt a scarf using the nuno method by laying multiple thin layers of differently coloured merino roving over silk organza. The layers blend during fulling into gradients and marled colour effects that cannot be produced by mixing roving before felting. The technique requires careful control of layer thickness and the order of colour placement to achieve the intended blend.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from the nuno-felting tradition established by Polly Stirling and developed in studio practice. Multi-layer colour blending over organza is standard advanced nuno technique taught in workshop and guild settings; this description is observation-led.

content.text: onto silk organza produces a lightweight, draped scarf in which the wool gathers the silk into pleats during
. Introducing multiple thin layers of differently coloured merino

before wetting allows the colours to blend during agitation rather than mixing before they go down. The result is a surface that shows gradients and marled areas where adjacent colours have migrated and overlapped. The key variables are layer order, layer thickness, and the zone in which each colour is concentrated. Thin individual layers are essential: thick deposits of a single colour compact without blending and produce hard-edged blocks rather than gradients.

items.name: Silk organza, natural or white, 90 by 30 cm
Merino roving, colour A (lightest or coolest)
Merino roving, colour C (darkest or warmest)
Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)
Felting net (same size or larger than the organza)

content.text: Step 1: Plan the colour zones. Before laying any roving, decide where each colour will be concentrated and where you want the blends to occur. A simple approach: colour A covers the left third of the scarf, colour C covers the right third, and colour B bridges the centre third. In the overlap zones, colours A and B share the same area in alternating wisps, and colours B and C overlap in the right-centre zone. Sketch the zones on paper first if it helps.

Step 2: Lay the first roving layer. Lay the silk organza flat on the felting mat. Pull very thin wisps of colour A and lay them across the left third of the organza running in one direction (lengthwise, for example). The wisps should be thin enough to see the silk weave beneath them. This first layer covers the left concentration zone and extends slightly into the centre zone, becoming progressively sparser as it moves right.

Step 3: Add the second colour layer. Without wetting, place thin wisps of colour C across the right third of the scarf, running in the same direction as colour A. In the right-centre blend zone, place colour C wisps sparsely over any colour B wisps that have already gone down. Extend colour C slightly into the centre so that the right edge of colour A's coverage zone and the left edge of colour C's coverage zone overlap by 5 to 10 cm in the centre.

Step 4: Add the bridging colour. Place thin wisps of colour B across the full centre zone, running at 90 degrees to the first two layers. The cross-direction placement means colour B will blend at right angles to A and C, which produces a slightly different visual blend from a layer running parallel. In the blend zones on both sides, colour B wisps should interleave with the edge wisps of A and C rather than covering them completely.

Step 5: Add optional accent layers. If you want marled accents (small flecks of a fourth colour or thin streaks of high contrast), place very sparse wisps of the accent colour now, concentrated in the blend zones. These will not fully merge and will remain as distinct flecks or streaks in the finished scarf. Keep accent wisps thin and irregular for a natural marled look.

Step 6: Wet and begin migration. Lay the felting net over the entire surface. Dissolve olive-oil soap in hot water and pour or spray it over the net in sections, saturating the roving without displacing it. Press the net down firmly across the whole surface. Rub through the net with flat hands in circular motions for 8 to 10 minutes, keeping the surface hot and adding more soapy water as needed. Migration begins when the wool fibres push through the organza weave and become visible on the underside.

Step 7: Check migration and continue rubbing. Lift one corner of the net and gently lift the organza. If fibres have migrated to the underside and the pinch test shows the roving does not pull away from the fabric cleanly, migration is underway. Replace the net and continue rubbing for a further 5 minutes. The colours in the blend zones will have begun to migrate toward each other; the gradient is forming inside the fabric at this stage.

Step 8: Full the scarf. Remove the net. Roll the scarf up in the tea towel and roll it firmly back and forth on the felting mat for 4 to 5 minutes. Unroll, turn the scarf 90 degrees, re-roll, and roll again. The organza will begin to pleat and gather as the wool contracts. Check the colour blend zones at each unrolling; the gradient should be softening and integrating. Continue until the scarf has gathered to approximately two-thirds of its starting length and the blend zones show a smooth colour transition.

Step 9: Rinse and shape. Rinse in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge into cold water for 30 to 60 seconds. The cold shock closes the fibre scales and firms the surface. Squeeze gently without wringing. Lay the scarf flat on a dry towel, shape the pleats by hand into the configuration you prefer, and leave to dry flat away from direct heat.

items.fix: Use thinner wisps and widen the overlap zone to at least 10 cm. Each layer should be translucent when held to a light. Thicker deposits of a single colour compact without blending.
items.cause: Individual roving layers were too thick, or the blend zones between colours were too narrow
items.problem: The colours appear as blocks rather than gradients

items.fix: In future, keep the darker colour's wisps especially thin in the blend zone. If the light colour has been fully obscured, add a sparse extra layer of the light roving to the affected area, wet it, and work it in from the top.
items.cause: The darker colour was placed in too thick a layer over the light colour's zone
items.problem: The lighter colour has been overwhelmed and is barely visible

items.fix: Rewet with very hot soapy water and rub more firmly through the net. Silk organza allows migration readily; the main cause of failure is cool water or insufficient pressure. Check that the organza weave is open enough: a very tight organza weave blocks migration.
items.cause: The water temperature dropped too much during work, or the soap concentration is too low
items.problem: The wool is not migrating through the organza after 15 minutes of rubbing

items.fix: Smooth any very thick spots before wetting by gently pulling wisps thinner. Once the scarf is wet, work the thick zones more during rubbing to even out the rate of felting. Uneven gathering is characteristic of the nuno technique and can be a feature; very severe puckering in one spot indicates a significantly heavier wool deposit there.
items.cause: The roving coverage was uneven across the surface, causing some zones to contract more than others
items.problem: The scarf is puckering unevenly rather than gathering smoothly

content.text: Hand-wash in cool water with a drop of wool-safe wash. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry flat in the gathered shape. If you prefer the pleats to be more pronounced, press the scarf flat while wet and let it dry that way. If the surface has any loose roving ends, trim with small sharp scissors or tuck them into the organza using a blunt darning needle. Silk organza is delicate; do not rub the surface when washing as the silk fibres abrade easily.
